_**User (2026-05-05 17:37:36Z)**_

here's the exisiting nginx config for my other app on my server. i don't need sse. do i need caching?:

upstream onboarding_app_upstream {
  # Default upstream strategy is round-robin.
  server 127.0.0.1:3001 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
  server 127.0.0.1:3002 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
  keepalive 64;
}

server {
  listen 80;
  listen [::]:80;
  server_name grnlght.co www.grnlght.co;

  return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}

server {
  listen 443 ssl http2;
  listen [::]:443 ssl http2;
  server_name grnlght.co www.grnlght.co;

  ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/cloudflare/origin.pem;
  ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/cloudflare/origin-key.pem;
  ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;

  include /etc/nginx/snippets/cloudflare-origin-access.conf;

  client_max_body_size 25m;

  # Notifications SSE endpoint.
  location = /api/notifications {
    proxy_pass http://onboarding_app_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header CF-Connecting-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
    proxy_set_header Connection "";

    proxy_buffering off;
    proxy_cache off;
    proxy_request_buffering off;
    proxy_read_timeout 86400;
    proxy_send_timeout 86400;
    chunked_transfer_encoding off;

    add_header X-Accel-Buffering no;
  }

  # Project timeline SSE endpoint.
  location ~ ^/api/projects/[^/]+/messages$ {
    proxy_pass http://onboarding_app_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header CF-Connecting-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
    proxy_set_header Connection "";

    proxy_buffering off;
    proxy_cache off;
    proxy_request_buffering off;
    proxy_read_timeout 86400;
    proxy_send_timeout 86400;
    chunked_transfer_encoding off;

    add_header X-Accel-Buffering no;
  }

  # Static assets are content-hashed by Vite, so cache aggressively.
  location /assets/ {
    proxy_pass http://onboarding_app_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header CF-Connecting-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;

    expires 1y;
    add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable";
  }

  location / {
    proxy_pass http://onboarding_app_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header CF-Connecting-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
    proxy_set_header Connection "";
    proxy_read_timeout 120;
    proxy_send_timeout 120;
  }
}

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Your existing config is solid, and for this portfolio app you can simplify it a lot: no SSE locations, likely a single upstream target, and targeted caching only for static files. I’ll give you a drop-in Nginx server block plus exactly what to cache and what not to cache behind Cloudflare.

Short answer: yes, use caching, but only for static assets.

For your React Router SSR app behind Cloudflare:
1. Cache hashed static assets aggressively.
2. Do not aggressively cache HTML/app routes.
3. Skip all SSE-specific proxy settings.

A clean config for this app would look like this:

~~~nginx
upstream portfolio_app_upstream {
  server 127.0.0.1:3000 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
  keepalive 32;
}

server {
  listen 80;
  listen [::]:80;
  server_name yourdomain.com www.yourdomain.com;
  return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}

server {
  listen 443 ssl http2;
  listen [::]:443 ssl http2;
  server_name yourdomain.com www.yourdomain.com;

  ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/cloudflare/origin.pem;
  ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/cloudflare/origin-key.pem;
  ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;

  include /etc/nginx/snippets/cloudflare-origin-access.conf;

  # Vite hashed assets: safe to cache long-term
  location /assets/ {
    proxy_pass http://portfolio_app_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header CF-Connecting-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;

    expires 1y;
    add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable";
  }

  # Public files that are usually not content-hashed (images, logos, etc.)
  location ~* \.(?:png|jpg|jpeg|gif|webp|svg|ico|css|js|woff2?)$ {
    proxy_pass http://portfolio_app_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header CF-Connecting-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;

    expires 7d;
    add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=604800";
  }

  # App routes / SSR responses
  location / {
    proxy_pass http://portfolio_app_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header CF-Connecting-IP $http_cf_connecting_ip;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;

    proxy_read_timeout 120;
    proxy_send_timeout 120;

    # Avoid stale HTML after deploys
    add_header Cache-Control "no-cache";
  }
}
~~~

What this means for your question:
1. Do you need caching? Strictly no, but you should enable it for static files because it improves speed and reduces server load.
2. Should you cache SSR pages? Usually no, not by default. Keep them fresh unless you explicitly implement page caching rules.
3. Do you need SSE config? No, remove all SSE blocks and buffering

_**User (2026-05-05 18:07:17Z)**_

do i need to create a separate origin server certificate for this site or can i reuse my old one?

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

You can reuse the old Cloudflare Origin Certificate only if its SANs already include the exact hostname(s) for this new site.

Practical rule:
1. Same domain family: If cert includes example.com and/or *.example.com, you can reuse it for matching hostnames under that domain.
2. Different apex domain: If old cert is for grnlght.co, it will not cover something like dariohunt.com unless that domain is explicitly listed in SANs.
3. Security best practice: Create a separate origin cert per site (or per domain) anyway, so key rotation/revocation is isolated.

So in your case, if this portfolio uses a different domain than your existing app, create a new origin certificate. If it is just another subdomain under the same covered domain, reusing is fine.

---

_**User (2026-05-05 18:19:39Z)**_

how can i list the users on my vps server?

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Use these commands on the VPS:

1. List all system users (from passwd):
```bash
cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd
```

2. List only “human” login users (UID >= 1000, excluding nobody):
```bash
awk -F: '$3>=1000 && $1!="nobody" {print $1}' /etc/passwd
```

3. Show users currently logged in:
```bash
who
```

4. Show users with sudo access:
```bash
getent group sudo
```

If you want a single clean report, run:
```bash
echo "== Login users =="; awk -F: '$3>=1000 && $1!="nobody"{print $1}' /etc/passwd; echo; echo "== Sudo group =="; getent group sudo; echo; echo "== Currently logged in =="; who
```

---

_**User (2026-05-05 18:21:51Z)**_

i had set up a github deploy user to handle my deployments to the server, i'd like to use that user again

_**User (2026-05-05 18:29:39Z)**_

do i need to give him permission to the new compose file in the portfolio directory?

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Short answer: yes, the deploy user needs access to it.

If your GitHub Action SSHs in as that deploy user and runs docker compose in the portfolio folder, that user must be able to:
1. Enter the directory
2. Read the compose file
3. Access any referenced files like .env
4. Run Docker commands (usually by being in the docker group)

Minimal safe setup:

sudo chgrp -R github-deploy /opt/portfolio
sudo chmod 750 /opt/portfolio
sudo chmod 640 /opt/portfolio/compose.yml

If there are more files in that folder the deploy user must read, use:

sudo chown -R github-deploy:github-deploy /opt/portfolio
sudo chmod -R u+rwX,go-rwx /opt/portfolio

And make sure Docker permission is in place:

sudo usermod -aG docker github-deploy

Then log out/in (or reboot session) so group membership applies.

Quick check as that user:

su - github-deploy
cd /opt/portfolio
docker compose config

If that command works, permissions are good.

---

_**User (2026-05-05 18:31:11Z)**_

explain this:

sudo chgrp -R github-deploy /opt/portfolio
sudo chmod 750 /opt/portfolio
sudo chmod 640 /opt/portfolio/compose.yml

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

1. `sudo chgrp -R github-deploy /opt/portfolio`  
Changes the group ownership of everything inside `/opt/portfolio` to the `github-deploy` group.  
Result: users in that group can get group-level access to files there.

2. `sudo chmod 750 /opt/portfolio`  
Sets directory permissions to:
- owner: `rwx` (7) = full access
- group: `r-x` (5) = can enter and list directory, but not write
- others: `---` (0) = no access

For a directory, execute (`x`) means “can traverse into it.”

3. `sudo chmod 640 /opt/portfolio/compose.yml`  
Sets file permissions to:
- owner: `rw-` (6) = read/write
- group: `r--` (4) = read only
- others: `---` (0) = no access

So the deploy user/group can read `compose.yml` (enough for `docker compose`), but cannot edit it unless they are the file owner.

One important nuance: if your deploy user is not the owner and needs to modify files in `/opt/portfolio`, this setup is too restrictive. For read-only deploys it is good; for workflows that edit files, you’d use different permissions/ownership.

---

_**User (2026-05-05 18:32:56Z)**_

so this doesn't remove my main user as the owner?

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Correct, those commands do not change the owner user.

- `chgrp` changes only the group, not the owner.

_**User (2026-05-05 18:57:33Z)**_

i need to create a script for the user to deploy

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Yes. Make the deploy user run one server-side script, then have GitHub Actions call that script.

Use this as your deploy script on the VPS:

~~~bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

APP_DIR="/opt/portfolio"
SERVICE_NAME="app"
IMAGE_REPO="ghcr.io/YOUR_GH_USERNAME/portfolio-site"
IMAGE_TAG="${1:-latest}"

echo "[deploy] Starting deploy for ${IMAGE_REPO}:${IMAGE_TAG}"
cd "${APP_DIR}"

# Optional: write the image tag into compose via env var if you use ${IMAGE_TAG} in compose.yml
export IMAGE_TAG

# Pull and restart only this service
docker compose pull "${SERVICE_NAME}"
docker compose up -d --no-deps "${SERVICE_NAME}"

# Cleanup old dangling layers
docker image prune -f

# Basic status output
docker compose ps
echo "[deploy] Completed"
~~~

Then do this once on the VPS:

~~~bash
sudo mkdir -p /opt/portfolio
sudo nano /opt/portfolio/deploy.sh
sudo chmod 750 /opt/portfolio/deploy.sh
sudo chown your-main-user:github-deploy /opt/portfolio/deploy.sh
~~~

Make sure deploy user can run it and Docker:

~~~bash
sudo usermod -aG docker github-deploy
sudo chgrp -R github-deploy /opt/portfolio
sudo chmod 750 /opt/portfolio
~~~

In GitHub Actions, call it like this in your SSH step:

~~~yaml
script: |
  echo "${{ secrets.GHCR_PAT }}" | docker login ghcr.io -u "${{ secrets.GHCR_USERNAME }}" --password-stdin
  /opt/portfolio/deploy.sh latest
~~~

If you want, I can also give you a safer version with rollback (keeps previous running container if health check fails).

_**User (2026-05-05 19:03:14Z)**_

this is my other app script:

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

APP_DIR="/opt/apps/onboarding-app-staging"
COMPOSE_FILE="docker-compose.staging.yml"
ENV_FILE=".env.staging"

# Only allow deployment actions in this directory
cd "$APP_DIR" || exit 1

if [ ! -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" ]; then
  echo "Missing $APP_DIR/$COMPOSE_FILE"
  exit 1
fi

if [ ! -f "$ENV_FILE" ]; then
  echo "Missing $APP_DIR/$ENV_FILE"
  exit 1
fi

# Update containers to latest image tags from GHCR
docker compose --env-file "$ENV_FILE" -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" pull app
docker compose --env-file "$ENV_FILE" -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" up -d --remove-orphans

# Basic local health check (adjust endpoint/port if needed)
for i in $(seq 1 20); do
  if curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:3003/api/health >/dev/null; then
    echo "Health check passed"
    break
  fi
  echo "Waiting for app health... ($i/20)"
  sleep 3
done

# Fail hard if app never became healthy
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:3003/api/health >/dev/null

# Prune dangling image layers (safe default)
docker image prune -f

_**User (2026-05-05 19:11:44Z)**_

i created a /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh, how do i give the github deploy permissions to run it?

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

I’ll give you the exact least-privilege permission setup so your deploy user can execute the script without broad sudo access, plus a quick validation command.Use one of these two safe patterns.

1. Best for least privilege: allow only this one command with sudo

On VPS, as your admin user, run:

```bash
sudo chown root:root /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh
sudo chmod 750 /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh

echo 'github-deploy ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh' | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio
sudo chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio
sudo visudo -cf /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio
```

Then in GitHub Actions, call:

```bash
sudo /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh
```

2. Simpler but broader: make deploy user execute directly (no sudoers entry)

```bash
sudo chown root:github-deploy /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh
sudo chmod 750 /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh
```

Then Actions can call:

```bash
/usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh
```

Recommended choice:
Use option 1 if you want tighter control and auditability.

Quick test as deploy user:

```bash
sudo -iu github-deploy
sudo -l
sudo /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh
```

---

_**User (2026-05-05 19:13:32Z)**_

what is this?:

echo 'github-deploy ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh' | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio
sudo chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio
sudo visudo -cf /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

It creates and validates a sudo rule that lets only `github-deploy` run exactly one command as root, without a password.

1.  
```bash
echo 'github-deploy ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh' | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio
```  
Writes a sudoers policy file containing:
- user: `github-deploy`
- host: `ALL`
- may run as: `(root)`
- password required: `NOPASSWD`
- allowed command: only `/usr/local/bin/portfolio-script.sh`

2.  
```bash
sudo chmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/github-deploy-portfolio
```  
Sets required secure permissions for sudoers include files (`read-only` for owner/group). This prevents sudo from rejecting it for unsafe permissions.

_**User (2026-05-05 19:18:43Z)**_

do i need to add the github_token?

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

No, you do not need to add a GITHUB_TOKEN secret manually.

GitHub Actions provides it automatically for each workflow run as secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN.

You only need to add your custom secrets:
1. VPS_HOST
2. VPS_USER
3. VPS_PORT
4. VPS_SSH_KEY
5. GHCR_USERNAME
6. GHCR_PAT

Why both tokens exist:
1. GITHUB_TOKEN is used in CI to push image to GHCR.
2. GHCR_PAT is used on your VPS to pull from GHCR during deploy.
